learning_objectives: Map the structure of the modern game industry from indie studios to AAA publishers
Identify the major career paths and roles within game development teams
Understand how games move from concept to release across different studio sizes
Analyze current industry trends including consolidation, subscription services, and remote work
readings: Blood, Sweat, and Pixels, Ch. 1-2 (Jason Schreier)
IGDA Developer Satisfaction Survey, latest edition

lecture_notes.introduction: The game industry generates over $180 billion annually and employs hundreds of thousands of professionals worldwide in roles spanning design, engineering, art, audio, production, quality assurance, marketing, and community management. Understanding the industry's structure, culture, business models, and career paths is essential for entering it prepared rather than naive. This course is your bridge between academic game development and professional practice. Over fifteen weeks, we will cover everything you need to navigate the industry successfully: building a portfolio that gets you hired, writing resumes that pass applicant tracking systems, managing projects with Agile methodology, understanding intellectual property law that protects your work, negotiating contracts, and recognizing workplace practices that are sustainable versus those that lead to burnout. The industry is undergoing rapid transformation through consolidation, the rise of subscription services, the growing importance of live-service models, and the normalization of remote work. Understanding these trends is not academic trivia -- they directly affect where jobs exist, what skills are valued, and what your career trajectory might look like.
key_concepts: Industry segments: AAA (200+ person teams, $50M+ budgets), AA (50-200 people, $5-50M), indie (1-20 people, self-funded), mobile, serious games, esports
Publisher-developer relationship models: first-party (platform-owned), second-party (exclusive partnership), third-party (independent), self-published indie
Key roles: design (game, level, systems, narrative), engineering (gameplay, engine, tools, network), art (concept, 3D, technical, VFX), audio, production, QA
Studio organizational structures: flat (indie), functional (department-based), matrix (project + department), and their communication patterns
Funding structures: publisher advance, venture capital, crowdfunding, grants, early access revenue, platform exclusivity deals
Regional industry hubs: North America (LA, Seattle, Austin, Montreal), Europe (London, Stockholm, Helsinki), Asia (Tokyo, Seoul, Shanghai)
Remote and distributed development: tools, challenges, and post-pandemic normalization
Industry consolidation: major acquisitions, impact on studio independence, implications for job seekers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive studio org chart exercise: mapping roles at a hypothetical 50-person studio, identifying dependencies and communication channels between departments
setup_requirements: LinkedIn account for professional networking exercises
Access to GDC Vault (free tier) for postmortem talks

review.value: AAA studios run 2-5 year development cycles with 100+ person teams and publisher funding, while indie studios operate with small teams, shorter cycles, and self-funding or grants
The most employable game developers have deep expertise in one discipline plus broad working knowledge across adjacent disciplines that makes them effective collaborators
Major acquisitions (Microsoft-Activision, Sony-Bungie, Embracer Group) are reshaping the industry landscape, reducing the number of independent publishers and concentrating IP ownership
Post-pandemic, many studios offer hybrid or fully remote positions. This expands geographic opportunity but requires stronger asynchronous communication skills

learning_objectives: Construct a portfolio that demonstrates relevant skills for specific target roles
Curate and present project work to maximize impact with hiring managers in first 30 seconds
Build a professional online presence across appropriate platforms
Avoid common portfolio mistakes that cause immediate rejection
readings: Game Industry Portfolio Best Practices guide (provided on LMS)
Selected portfolio reviews from game industry hiring managers (links on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Your portfolio is the single most important asset in your job search. It is what hiring managers look at before deciding whether to spend 30 minutes interviewing you, and if it does not immediately demonstrate relevant skills, they move to the next candidate. Hiring managers spend 15-30 seconds on a portfolio before deciding whether to continue or close the tab. This means your portfolio must communicate your strongest work, your specific skills, and your professional potential within that window. Today we focus on portfolio construction as strategic communication. Different roles require fundamentally different approaches. A designer's portfolio showcases design thinking through documents, prototypes, and case studies. A programmer's portfolio highlights code quality through GitHub repositories and technical write-ups. An artist's portfolio leads with visual impact through polished pieces with process documentation. A producer's portfolio demonstrates organizational skills through project timelines and postmortems. We will review three portfolios live -- one strong, one average, one weak -- and identify exactly what separates them.
key_concepts: Portfolio structure: hero section with strongest work, project case studies with depth, about page with personality, clear contact information
Discipline-specific requirements: design (GDDs, prototypes), programming (GitHub, code samples), art (polished pieces, process), production (timelines, postmortems)
Case study format: problem statement, your specific role, process, solution, measurable outcome, lessons learned
Platform options: personal website (most professional), ArtStation (art standard), GitHub (programming essential), itch.io (game uploads)
Common mistakes: too many mediocre pieces, no context for collaborative work, broken links, outdated projects, inconsistent quality
SEO and discoverability: domain name, meta descriptions, being findable when recruiters search your name
Portfolio maintenance: updating quarterly, removing weaker pieces, keeping all links functional
Personal branding consistency: same photo, consistent bio, aligned messaging across all platforms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live review of three game industry portfolios with group critique identifying specific strengths, weaknesses, and improvement priorities
setup_requirements: Screenshots and documentation from at least two completed projects
Web hosting account or portfolio platform account

review.value: Hiring managers want to see process documentation, iteration, and problem-solving alongside finished results. A polished piece with a case study showing the journey beats a polished piece with no context.
Five strong pieces with context outperform twenty mediocre pieces. Your weakest piece defines your perceived skill level in the reviewer's mind.
If a hiring manager cannot identify your discipline, skill level, and strongest work within 30 seconds, your portfolio fails regardless of content quality.
Every piece in your portfolio should be relevant to the role you are targeting. A game designer portfolio with web design work dilutes the message.

learning_objectives: Write a game-industry-targeted resume that passes ATS filters and engages human reviewers
Prepare for common interview formats: phone screens, portfolio reviews, design tests, whiteboard coding
Develop structured responses to behavioral and technical questions using the STAR method
Understand salary negotiation fundamentals and industry compensation benchmarks
readings: Ask a Game Dev blog, interview preparation series (provided on LMS)
Gamasutra, 'What We Look For When Hiring' collection

lecture_notes.introduction: Getting hired in the game industry requires navigating multiple interview stages, each testing different competencies. The process typically starts with an ATS scan -- if the right keywords are not present in clean formatting, no human sees your application. Then a recruiter screens your resume for 15-30 seconds. Then a phone screen tests communication and basic qualification. Then discipline-specific evaluation: programmers get coding challenges, designers get design tests, artists get art tests. Finally, an on-site interview loop tests technical skills, cultural fit, and collaboration. Preparation is the strongest predictor of success at every stage. Today we systematically prepare for each stage and practice the skill most candidates handle poorly: salary negotiation. Understanding industry compensation data and negotiating confidently can mean tens of thousands of dollars in starting salary.
key_concepts: Resume format: one page maximum, accomplishment-driven bullet points with quantified results, professional email address
ATS optimization: keywords from job postings, standard section headers, clean formatting without tables or graphics that confuse parsers
Interview types: behavioral (STAR method), technical (live coding, system design), portfolio walkthrough (5-minute structured presentation)
Design test strategies: scope management, documentation quality, time-boxing ruthlessly
Phone screen preparation: research company, prepare 3 questions about the role, practice 2-minute introduction
Salary negotiation: research market rates, negotiate total compensation, never give the first number if avoidable
Follow-up protocol: thank-you emails within 24 hours with specific conversation references
Red flags in postings: 'passion' as compensation, extremely broad roles, no work-life balance mention
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mock interview session: instructor conducts a live portfolio review with a volunteer, followed by group debrief on technique
setup_requirements: Current resume draft for in-class workshop
One project ready for 5-minute portfolio walkthrough
STAR method worksheet with 5 prepared behavioral answers

review.value: Situation, Task, Action, Result: a structured framework for answering behavioral interview questions with specific, measurable examples from your experience.
Studios evaluate design test submissions on clarity of thought and documentation quality, not on how much content you cram in. Scoping well is part of the test.
Applicant Tracking Systems scan resumes for keywords and formatting. Tables, graphics, and creative headers that look good to humans are invisible or garbled to ATS parsers.
Salary is only part of compensation. Benefits, bonuses, equity, relocation, remote work flexibility, and PTO all have monetary value. Negotiate the full package.

class_schedule.topic: Project Management: Agile and Scrum in Game Development
learning_objectives: Apply Agile and Scrum methodologies to game development workflows
Manage a product backlog and plan sprints for a game project with realistic estimation
Identify how game studios adapt standard Agile practices to creative production
Facilitate effective sprint ceremonies: planning, standups, reviews, retrospectives
readings: Agile Game Development with Scrum, Ch. 1-4 (Clinton Keith)
GDC Talk: 'Agile at Riot Games' postmortem (GDC Vault)

lecture_notes.introduction: Most game studios use some form of Agile methodology, but pure Scrum rarely survives contact with creative production. Game development involves discovery, iteration, and creative exploration that do not fit neatly into fixed sprint commitments. Studios adapt Agile to handle this: they use creative spikes for uncertain work, separate predictable production from open-ended exploration, and adjust commitment strategies for creative unpredictability. Today we learn both the standard framework and the studio-specific adaptations. We practice core ceremonies: sprint planning, daily standups, sprint reviews, and retrospectives. These are the communication rituals that keep teams aligned and projects on track. A well-run retrospective that identifies and fixes a systemic problem is worth more than any individual feature implementation.
key_concepts: Scrum roles in games: product owner (creative director), scrum master (producer), development team (cross-discipline pod)
Sprint planning for creative work: separating predictable tasks from uncertain tasks and estimating accordingly
User stories for games: player-facing stories vs technical stories with clear acceptance criteria
Estimation techniques: story points (relative sizing), planning poker (team consensus), T-shirt sizing, reference story calibration
Creative spikes: time-boxed exploration tasks (2-3 days) for investigating unknowns before committing to implementation
Velocity tracking: measuring story points completed per sprint to improve estimation accuracy over time
Retrospectives: structured formats (Start/Stop/Continue, 4Ls, Sailboat) for productive team reflection
Common Agile failures: scope commitment without creative flexibility, velocity as performance metric, ceremony fatigue
lecture_notes.demonstration: Sprint planning exercise: teams break down a hypothetical game feature into user stories, estimate with planning poker, and plan a two-week sprint

review.value: Sprints are short fixed-length iterations (1-4 weeks) with adjustable scope, while milestones are fixed-scope targets (alpha, beta, gold) with adjustable timelines.
A time-boxed exploration task used in game Agile to investigate unknowns (art style tests, mechanic prototypes) before committing to full production.
The average number of story points a team completes per sprint. Used for planning (not performance evaluation). Velocity naturally stabilizes after 3-4 sprints.
A team-agreed checklist that defines when a user story is complete: code reviewed, tested, merged, art approved, design verified. Prevents 90%-done stories from accumulating.

learning_objectives: Distinguish between copyright, trademark, patent, and trade secret protections for games
Identify key clauses in employment and contractor agreements affecting work ownership
Recognize when to seek legal counsel for IP and contract issues
Understand licensing models for assets, middleware, and open-source components
readings: The Game Developer's Guide to IP Law (selected chapters)
US Copyright Office, 'Copyright Basics' circular

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding intellectual property law protects both your creative work and your career. Every game developer encounters IP issues: the employment contract determining who owns your work, the asset license governing your font usage, the copyright question about mechanic similarity, and the trademark concern about game naming. Legal ignorance is not a defense. A cease-and-desist over trademark conflict can derail a launch. An overly broad IP assignment clause can prevent personal projects. A misunderstood open-source license can force source code release. Today we learn the fundamentals -- not to become lawyers, but to recognize issues early, ask the right questions, and know when professional counsel is needed.
key_concepts: Copyright: automatic protection for creative expression (code, art, audio, text) but NOT for ideas, mechanics, or rules
Trademark: protection for names, logos, and distinctive identifiers associated with your brand
Patents in games: rare but real (Namco loading screen patent, Warner Bros. Nemesis system). Expensive to file and enforce.
Trade secrets: proprietary information protected through confidentiality, not registration
Work-for-hire: employee work belongs to employer; contractor work belongs to contractor unless written assignment agreement exists
Key contract clauses: IP assignment, non-compete, non-disclosure, royalty structures, and their negotiation points
Asset licensing: royalty-free, Creative Commons varieties, proprietary licenses and what each actually permits
Open-source licenses: MIT (permissive), GPL (copyleft -- requires source release), Apache (permissive with patent grant)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Contract clause analysis: reviewing a sample game developer employment agreement, identifying clauses that affect developer rights, with instructor commentary on negotiation points
setup_requirements: Sample contracts provided on LMS (anonymized real agreements)

review.value: Under US copyright law, work created by an employee within scope of employment belongs to the employer. Contractors retain ownership unless a written agreement assigns it.
Copyright protects specific expression (code, art, audio, text) but not underlying rules or mechanics. Genre clones are legal; asset theft is not.
Restrict your ability to work for competitors after leaving. Enforceability varies by state (unenforceable in California). Always negotiate scope and duration.
If you use GPL-licensed code in your game, you may be required to release your entire game's source code under GPL. Understand license compatibility before integrating any open-source library.

learning_objectives: Analyze systemic causes of crunch culture with evidence from industry research
Evaluate studio practices that promote sustainable development
Develop personal strategies for maintaining work-life balance
Understand labor law and organizing trends in game development
readings: Schreier, 'The Horrible World of Video Game Crunch' (Kotaku)
IGDA Quality of Life White Paper, latest edition

lecture_notes.introduction: Crunch is the game industry's most persistent labor issue. Sustained overtime beyond standard working hours, often unpaid or culturally coerced. The 2024 IGDA survey found 60% of developers reported crunch in the past two years, 35% lasting more than two months. The human cost is documented: burnout, depression, relationship strain, physical health problems, and paradoxically, increased error rates. Research on sustained overtime is unambiguous: productivity per hour declines after 50 hours, and after two consecutive weeks of 60+ hours, total output is typically lower than sustainable hours would produce. Crunch is not just unethical -- it is inefficient. Yet it persists because of structural incentives: fixed dates with unfixed scope, hero culture, poor estimation, and labor surplus making workers feel replaceable. Today we examine these systemic causes, study studios that eliminated crunch successfully, and develop personal strategies for recognizing and responding to unsustainable expectations.
key_concepts: Defining crunch: sustained overtime, often unpaid or culturally coerced through peer pressure and career fear
Root causes: scope creep, poor estimation, hero culture, misaligned incentives (bonuses tied to ship dates)
Health impacts: burnout, depression, cardiovascular risk, reduced cognitive function after sustained sleep deprivation
Productivity research: output per hour declines after 50 hrs/week, total output plateaus at 55 hrs, sustained 60+ hours produces LESS than 40 after 3-4 weeks
Studios avoiding crunch: Supergiant Games (strict 40-hour weeks), Media Molecule, Insomniac (improved post-Sunset Overdrive)
Scope management as prevention: disciplined pre-production scoping is the most effective crunch prevention
Personal strategies: boundary setting, career diversification, recognizing warning signs, knowing when to leave
Labor organizing: Game Workers Alliance, unionization trends, collective bargaining for conditions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study discussion: Rockstar Games RDR2 crunch controversy -- timeline, studio response, industry impact, and systemic changes that could have prevented it
setup_requirements: Studio evaluation checklist for job seekers

review.value: Research consistently shows sustained work beyond 50 hours produces diminishing returns, with output per hour declining and error rates increasing after the second consecutive week.
The most effective crunch prevention is disciplined scope management during pre-production, making it a production leadership responsibility rather than an individual developer problem.
When visible sacrifice (staying late, working weekends) is rewarded with promotions and praise, it creates an incentive structure that perpetuates crunch regardless of productivity evidence.
Ask prospective employers: 'When was the last crunch period, how long did it last, and what changes were made to prevent the next one?' The answer reveals culture.

learning_objectives: Understand the publishing landscape: self-publishing, indie publishers, and traditional deals
Evaluate publishing agreements and identify critical contract terms
Navigate platform distribution requirements for Steam, consoles, and mobile
Plan a release strategy including timing, pricing, and platform selection
readings: The Game Developer's Business Handbook (excerpts)
Steam Direct Documentation and Steamworks Partner Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Publishing a game is as much a business challenge as a creative one. Today's developers have more options than ever: self-publishing through Steam Direct and console programs, indie publishers like Devolver Digital, or traditional publisher deals. Each model has distinct trade-offs in creative freedom, financial risk, marketing support, and revenue share. A self-published Steam game keeps 70% of revenue but bears 100% of marketing costs. A publisher deal might fund development entirely but take 50-70% of revenue and retain IP rights. We also cover platform mechanics: Steam's review process, console certification, App Store guidelines, and release timing strategies.
key_concepts: Self-publishing: full creative control, 100% risk, retain IP, 70% Steam revenue, requires own marketing
Indie publishers: partial funding, revenue share (50/50 to 70/30 dev-favorable), may retain IP, selective partnerships
Traditional publishers: full funding, established marketing, publisher often retains IP, developer gets royalties after recoup
Key contract terms: advance/recoup, revenue share, IP ownership, sequel rights, platform exclusivity, termination clauses
Steam distribution: Steamworks, store page optimization, wishlists as predictor, sales participation, review management
Console certification: TRC/TCR checklists, submission process, patch timelines, first-party developer programs
Mobile distribution: App Store Review Guidelines, Google Play policies, regional requirements, IAP regulations
Release timing: avoiding major launches, leveraging showcases (Next Fest), seasonal buying patterns
lecture_notes.demonstration: Publishing deal comparison: analyzing three offers for a hypothetical game and recommending which to accept with financial and strategic justification

review.value: Gross sales -> platform fee (30%) -> publisher recoup -> publisher share -> developer royalties. A $30 game might net the developer $8-12 per unit through this chain.
Publishers advance development funds that must be repaid from revenue before the developer receives royalties. Until the advance is recouped, the developer receives nothing.
Steam wishlists are the single best predictor of launch sales. Benchmark: 7,000+ wishlists for viable indie launch. Average day-1 conversion: 10-15% of total wishlists.
The most important long-term term in any publishing deal. Losing IP ownership means the publisher can make sequels, merchandise, and adaptations without your involvement or additional compensation.

learning_objectives: Design a comprehensive QA plan covering functional, performance, and compliance testing
Understand QA team structure, bug tracking workflows, and triage processes
Write effective bug reports that enable efficient resolution
Evaluate QA tools and automation strategies for different project scales
readings: Game Testing by Charles Schultz and Robert Bryant, Ch. 1-5
ISTQB Foundation Syllabus applied to game testing

lecture_notes.introduction: Quality assurance is chronically undervalued despite being the last defense between your game and the player's experience. A single critical launch bug can tank reviews and trigger refund waves. Understanding QA as a professional discipline is essential for everyone in development. Designers need testability awareness, programmers need to write testable code, producers need to plan QA resources accurately. Today we cover QA comprehensively: test planning, bug reporting, triage processes, automation strategies, and the growing recognition that QA is a career discipline worthy of respect and fair compensation.
key_concepts: QA plan structure: test scope, test matrix (features x platforms), entry/exit criteria, resource allocation, risk prioritization
Testing types: functional, regression, performance, compatibility, compliance (platform TRC/TCR), localization
Bug report anatomy: title, reproduction steps, expected vs actual behavior, severity, priority, environment, evidence
Bug triage: team reviews, classifies severity/priority, assigns ownership, schedules fix, verifies resolution
QA team structure: QA lead, functional testers, performance analysts, automation engineers, compliance specialists
Test automation: unit tests, integration tests, screenshot comparison, performance benchmarks, smoke tests
Automation limitations: cannot test fun, cannot evaluate visual quality subjectively, cannot replicate full player behavior space
QA career paths: tester -> lead -> manager -> director, or lateral to production, design, or technical roles
lecture_notes.demonstration: Bug writing workshop: given video of a buggy game build, write professional bug reports for 5 issues, then peer-review for clarity and completeness
setup_requirements: Bug tracking tool access (Jira or similar)

review.value: Severity: how bad is the bug? (crash > major > minor > cosmetic). Priority: how urgently must it be fixed? (blocker > high > medium > low). A cosmetic bug on the title screen might be low severity but high priority.
Re-testing previously working features after code changes to verify nothing was broken. Automated regression suites catch unintended side effects of new code.
A quick, automated test suite run on every new build to verify basic functionality before committing to full testing. Catches build-breaking issues in minutes.
Console manufacturers require games to pass Technical Requirement Checklists before release. Failure means rejection and re-submission, potentially delaying launch by weeks.

learning_objectives: Apply conflict resolution strategies to common game development disputes
Facilitate productive meetings that respect time and produce outcomes
Communicate effectively across disciplines with appropriate vocabulary
Build team culture that supports both productivity and wellbeing
readings: Radical Candor by Kim Scott, Ch. 1-3
GDC Talk: 'Building Great Teams' by Jesse Schell

lecture_notes.introduction: The game industry's most complex challenge is not rendering or networking -- it is getting talented people with different specializations, vocabularies, and creative visions to collaborate effectively. A team of brilliant individuals who cannot communicate across discipline boundaries will ship a worse game than a team of competent individuals who collaborate seamlessly. Today we develop communication and leadership skills through practice: conflict resolution with real game development scenarios, meeting facilitation techniques, feedback delivery using the SBI model, and team culture building.
key_concepts: Cross-discipline communication: explaining technical constraints in non-technical language and creative vision in technical terms
Conflict resolution: four types (task, process, relationship, status) and appropriate strategies for each
Meeting facilitation: agenda-setting, time-boxing, parking lot for tangents, action items with owners, async-first decisions
Feedback delivery: SBI model (Situation, Behavior, Impact), radical candor, avoiding the feedback sandwich trap
Psychological safety: environments where team members can raise concerns and admit mistakes without fear
Remote team communication: synchronous vs asynchronous norms, documentation culture, time zone management
Onboarding: reducing ramp-up time through documentation, mentorship pairing, structured first-week experiences
Burnout signals: changes in communication, quality decline, withdrawal, cynicism about the project
lecture_notes.demonstration: Role-play: teams practice resolving a scope disagreement between design and engineering using structured conflict resolution, followed by debrief

review.value: The shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking. Members can speak up, admit mistakes, and disagree without fear of humiliation. Essential for catching problems early.

Situation (when/where), Behavior (what specifically happened), Impact (the effect it had). 'In yesterday's review (S), when you dismissed the art concern without discussion (B), the artist stopped contributing ideas (I).'
Default to asynchronous communication (written, documented) and reserve synchronous time (meetings) for decisions that require real-time discussion. Respects everyone's deep work time.

Task conflict (disagreeing about design decisions) is healthy and produces better outcomes. Relationship conflict (personal animosity) is destructive. The skill is keeping the first from becoming the second.

learning_objectives: Analyze game studio financial structure including revenue streams and cost structures
Build a project budget from pre-production through post-launch support
Evaluate studio business models: work-for-hire, owned IP, publisher-funded, self-funded
Understand how financial pressures affect creative decisions and team welfare
readings: The Game Developer's Business Handbook (continued)
GDC Business Track: Studio financial management talks

lecture_notes.introduction: Every creative decision exists within a financial context. Your scope is constrained by budget, team size by funding, timeline by runway. Understanding studio economics means making informed creative arguments within realistic constraints. A designer proposing a $500K feature without understanding the budget is uninformed, not creative. Today we open the books: how much games cost (more than most students expect), how revenue flows through platform fees and publisher recoup, and why many studios fail despite making good games.
key_concepts: Development costs: indie ($200K-$2M), AA ($5M-$50M), AAA ($50M-$300M+), marketing often equals development cost
Revenue waterfall: gross -> platform fee (30%) -> publisher recoup -> publisher share -> developer royalties
Studio models: work-for-hire (stable, no IP), owned IP (high risk/reward), hybrid (WFH stability + IP upside)
Project budgeting: staffing (70-80%), outsourcing, middleware, hardware, marketing, QA, localization, contingency (10-15%)
Cash flow: the gap between development spending and revenue, and runway requirements
Team scaling: full-time vs contractor trade-offs, hiring costs vs crunch costs
Post-launch economics: DLC cost vs revenue, live-service staffing, server costs, customer support
Studio failure patterns: over-scoping first project, under-budgeting marketing, unfavorable publisher terms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Budget building exercise: creating a complete project budget for a hypothetical 18-month indie game with a 10-person team

review.value: The number of months a studio can operate without revenue. Calculate: total available cash / monthly burn rate. Studios need runway through launch plus 3-6 months for revenue to arrive.
A $20 Steam game: $14 after platform fee. If publisher recoup applies, developer may receive $0 for thousands of units. After recoup with 50/50 split: $7 per unit to developer.
Industry rule of thumb: marketing budget equals 50-100% of development budget for premium games. A $500K game needs $250-500K in marketing to be discoverable.
Studios that combine owned-IP projects (high risk, high reward) with work-for-hire contracts (stable revenue) can weather the unpredictability of game sales.

learning_objectives: Develop a marketing strategy for an indie game from announcement through post-launch
Build and maintain community using appropriate platforms and engagement practices
Understand the relationship between wishlists, media coverage, streamer engagement, and sales
Create effective marketing assets: trailers, screenshots, store pages, press kits
readings: Chris Zukowski: How to Market a Game (selected articles)
GDC Talk: 'Marketing Fundamentals for Indie Developers'

lecture_notes.introduction: The most common cause of commercial failure for indie games is not quality -- it is obscurity. Over 14,000 new games release on Steam annually. Without deliberate marketing, even excellent games are invisible. Marketing for game developers is about consistently communicating what makes your game special to people most likely to care, starting months before release. Today we cover the complete marketing timeline from pre-production audience identification through post-launch community sustenance. We focus on the metrics that predict success: wishlists, social engagement quality, press and streamer coverage, and demo conversion rates.
key_concepts: Marketing timeline: pre-production (audience research) -> production (social media, devlogs) -> pre-launch (wishlists, press, demos) -> launch (reviews, streaming) -> post-launch (updates, sales)
Steam wishlists: primary launch predictor. Benchmark: 7,000+ for viable launch. Day-1 conversion: 10-15%. Build 6-12 months before launch.
Trailer creation: hook in first 3 seconds, show gameplay, communicate core appeal, 60-90 seconds max, professional audio
Store page optimization: capsule image, short description, tags, screenshots, trailer placement, review management
Press and streamer outreach: press kit (fact sheet, screenshots, trailer, key art), personalized emails, timing for impact
Community platforms: Discord (direct communication), Twitter/X (reach), Reddit (discussion), TikTok (viral potential)
Festival strategy: Steam Next Fest (essential for wishlists), showcase events, conventions, demo quality requirements
Post-launch: update announcements, seasonal sale participation, community events, franchise building
lecture_notes.demonstration: Marketing plan workshop: developing a 12-month plan for a hypothetical indie game with timeline, channels, content calendar, and wishlist targets

review.value: Steam wishlists are the single best predictor of launch week sales. Median conversion rate is 10-15% on day one. 50,000 wishlists predicts approximately 5,000-7,500 first-week sales.
Press coverage and streamer/YouTuber content you did not pay for. The most cost-effective marketing channel. Requires a genuinely interesting game and professional outreach.
A multi-day event where players try free demos. The highest-ROI marketing opportunity for indie developers. A good demo can generate thousands of wishlists in a week.
An audience passively consumes your content. A community actively engages, provides feedback, creates content, and advocates for your game. Build community, not just followers.

class_schedule.topic: Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion in Game Development
learning_objectives: Analyze the current state of diversity in the game industry using data
Identify systemic barriers limiting participation of underrepresented groups
Design inclusive hiring practices and studio cultures
Understand how diverse teams produce better games through broader perspectives
readings: IGDA Developer Satisfaction Survey: Diversity sections
Game Developer Diversity Report (provided)

lecture_notes.introduction: The game industry has a diversity problem affecting both who makes games and what games get made. Women represent approximately 30% of developers but less than 15% of senior leadership. Black and Hispanic developers are significantly underrepresented. These disparities matter as product quality issues: homogeneous teams have blind spots resulting in narrow appeal, tone-deaf content, and missed markets. Today we examine the data, identify systemic barriers, and discuss concrete actions for building inclusive workplaces and products.
key_concepts: Industry demographics: gender (~30% women), race/ethnicity (significant underrepresentation), age (concentrated 25-40)
Pipeline barriers: lack of career exposure, expensive education, unpaid internships, geographic studio concentration
Hiring bias: resume screening bias, interview bias (culture fit as homogeneity), portfolio bias against non-traditional backgrounds
Workplace barriers: harassment, bro culture, crunch disproportionately affecting caregivers, lack of mentorship
Business case: broader player perspective, reduced blind spots, larger addressable market, better decision-making (research-supported)
Inclusive hiring: blind resume review, structured interviews, diverse panels, alternative assessments, paid internships
Studio culture: code of conduct with enforcement, ERGs, inclusive benefits, representation in leadership
Individual actions: allyship, challenging exclusionary behavior, mentoring, evaluating studios on diversity before accepting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop: reviewing a hypothetical studio's hiring process and workplace policies, identifying diversity barriers, proposing actionable improvements

review.value: The shortage of diverse candidates entering game development is caused by lack of early exposure, prohibitive education costs, and cultural messaging that games are 'for' certain demographics.
'Culture fit' hiring often means 'people like us' -- perpetuating homogeneity. Replace with 'culture add': what unique perspectives does this candidate bring that we currently lack?
People hold multiple identities simultaneously. A Black woman in game development faces different challenges than a white woman or a Black man. Inclusive policies must address intersecting barriers.

Research from Harvard Business Review shows diverse teams make better decisions 87% of the time compared to homogeneous teams. In game development, this translates to games that appeal to broader audiences.

class_schedule.topic: Entrepreneurship and Starting a Game Studio
learning_objectives: Evaluate the decision to start a studio versus joining an existing one
Understand legal, financial, and operational requirements for studio formation
Build a business plan including funding strategy, team structure, and first project scope
Navigate common pitfalls that cause new studios to fail
readings: The Game Developer's Business Handbook, entrepreneurship chapters
Rami Ismail: Leveling the Playing Field talks and articles

lecture_notes.introduction: Starting your own studio is the dream of many developers and the graveyard of many savings accounts. The failure rate is high -- 80-90% of new studios close within five years. Primary causes are predictable: underfunding, poor scope, team conflicts, unrealistic timelines, and neglecting business while focusing on development. But for those who navigate successfully, studio ownership offers creative freedom, financial upside, and cultural control. Today we cover the practical reality: legal formation, financial planning, team building, business planning, and the common pitfalls.
key_concepts: Studio formation: LLC (simple, limited liability), corporation (investor-friendly). State of incorporation matters.
Co-founder agreements: equity split, vesting schedule (4-year with 1-year cliff), roles, decision authority, departure terms
Funding strategies: bootstrapping, grants (Epic MegaGrants), publisher deals, crowdfunding, angel investors
Financial planning: minimum 18-24 months runway, personal burn rate, studio overhead (insurance, tools, legal, accounting)
First project scoping: start smaller than you think. First game should take 6-12 months, not 3 years. Prove viability before scaling.
Team building: complementary co-founder skills (design + programming + art), contractor vs employee decisions
Common failures: underfunding, over-scoping, co-founder disputes, neglecting business operations, founder burnout
Sustainability: diversifying revenue (IP + work-for-hire), building pipeline efficiency, planning project two before project one ships
lecture_notes.demonstration: Business plan workshop: one-page plan for a hypothetical studio including funding, first project scope, team structure, and 2-year financial projection
setup_requirements: Co-founder agreement template (for review, not legal advice)

review.value: Co-founder equity vests over 4 years with a 1-year cliff. If a co-founder leaves after 6 months, they get nothing. After 1 year, 25%. This protects remaining founders from early departures.
Your first game should be small enough to ship in 6-12 months with current funding. Proving you can ship a complete game is more valuable than an impressive but unfinished dream project.
Total available cash divided by monthly expenses (salaries + overhead + tools + services). If runway is 12 months and the project takes 18, you will run out of money before launch.
Many successful indie studios fund their original IP development by taking work-for-hire contracts (porting, DLC development, tool creation) that provide stable revenue between releases.

class_schedule.topic: Career Strategy and Continuous Professional Development
learning_objectives: Develop a 5-year career strategy with milestones and contingency plans
Build professional relationships through strategic networking
Identify skill gaps through self-assessment and plan development activities
Navigate career transitions between roles, studio sizes, and employment models
readings: Career Development in the Game Industry (provided compilation)
GDC Career Development talks (viewing list)

lecture_notes.introduction: Your game industry career will last 30-40 years if managed well. The decisions you make in the first 5 years compound over decades. Strategic career development means having clear goals, understanding paths to those goals, actively developing required skills, and building relationships that create opportunities. Today we develop personal career strategies through honest self-assessment, path mapping, networking planning, and transition preparation. Real networking is about building mutual value relationships through shared interests and consistent connection, not awkward card exchanges.
key_concepts: Career path mapping: entry -> mid -> senior -> lead -> director with discipline-specific milestones and timelines
T-shaped skill development: deep expertise in primary discipline plus broad competence in adjacent areas
Self-assessment: honestly evaluating strengths, weaknesses, interests, and values to identify matching roles
Networking as relationship building: contributing to communities, attending events, offering help, maintaining connections
Conference strategy: GDC, industry events, local meetups, online communities, game jams as networking
Mentorship: finding mentors (specific questions, not 'be my mentor'), being a mentor (teaching solidifies knowledge)
Career transitions: indie to AAA (portfolio adaptation), AAA to indie (wearing multiple hats), discipline changes
Continuous learning: staying current through courses, reading, conferences, personal projects
lecture_notes.demonstration: Career planning workshop: each student develops a 5-year strategy with target roles, required skills, networking plan, and contingency scenarios

review.value: Deep expertise in one discipline (vertical bar) plus broad working knowledge across adjacent areas (horizontal bar). The most employable and effective team members in game development.
The most effective networkers build relationships by helping others before asking for help. Share knowledge, make introductions, contribute to communities. Reciprocity follows naturally.
A career plan is a compass, not a GPS route. Set directional goals but adapt to opportunities and changes. The value is in the thinking process, not rigid adherence to the plan.
GDC is the single highest-ROI investment for game industry career development. The talks are valuable but the hallway conversations, parties, and meetups are where careers are made.

learning_objectives: Present a comprehensive career readiness package demonstrating preparation
Provide constructive feedback on peer portfolios and career materials
Reflect on course learning and connect to personal career goals
Articulate a professional identity and value proposition to potential employers
readings: Review all course materials
Peer portfolio materials for review

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session is your professional debut. Each student presents their complete career readiness package: portfolio, resume, career strategy, and a 3-minute professional pitch answering 'Tell me about yourself.' This is a dress rehearsal for the conversations you will have as you enter the industry. Peer feedback simulates the portfolio review process, giving candid assessment before you stake your career on these materials. Beyond presentations, we take stock of what you have learned and what you still need to develop. No course fully prepares you for a career, but this course gives you frameworks for continued learning: evaluating studios, negotiating contracts, managing projects, protecting your IP and health, and building a career with intention.
key_concepts: Professional pitch: 2-3 minute structured introduction covering background, skills, projects, goals, and team contribution
Portfolio peer review: structured feedback on presentation, content quality, navigation, professional impression
Career readiness self-assessment across technical skills, professional materials, industry knowledge, sustainability
Industry trends: AI-assisted development, cloud gaming, spatial computing, consolidation, regulatory changes
Professional identity: defining what kind of developer you are, what you value, what you are known for
Continuous development: learning habits, conference plans, community engagement, personal projects
Support networks: classmate connections, professional organizations, mentorship relationships
First 90 days: establishing yourself at a new studio, building relationships, demonstrating value
lecture_notes.demonstration: Student career readiness presentations with structured peer feedback, followed by group reflection and professional commitments

review.value: A concise 30-second introduction for networking: your name, discipline, current work, and what you are looking for. Should feel natural, not rehearsed, and invite follow-up conversation.
The intersection of your skills, values, and what you want to build. A clear identity helps you make career decisions and communicate your value to others.
The most important period at a new job. Listen more than you talk. Learn the existing systems before proposing changes. Build trust through reliable execution of assigned work.
The game industry rewards sustained excellent work over decades. Avoid burning out in year 3. Invest in skills that compound over time. Build relationships that last longer than any single project.

quizzes.topic: Industry Structure, Portfolios, and Interview Preparation

questions.question: A junior game designer is preparing their portfolio for both AAA and indie applications. Describe three specific ways the portfolio should differ between these targets and explain why each difference matters.
questions.answer: 1) Scope of work: AAA portfolios highlight specific contributions within large teams; indie portfolios demonstrate breadth. 2) Documentation depth: AAA includes detailed design docs; indie emphasizes rapid prototyping. 3) Tool proficiency: AAA demonstrates industry-standard tools (Unreal, Jira); indie shows versatility and custom solutions.

rubric.key: Clear reasoning connecting each to hiring context
Understanding of AAA vs indie workflows

questions.question: Write a resume bullet point using accomplishment-driven format for: lead programmer on a 4-person student game, built in Unity over 12 weeks, won Best Gameplay at school showcase.
questions.answer: Led gameplay programming for a 4-person team over 12 weeks in Unity, architecting core combat and AI systems that earned Best Gameplay recognition at the annual showcase judged by industry professionals.

rubric.key: Uses active verb and specifies leadership role

questions.question: What does ATS stand for and why does it matter? Name two resume formatting practices that improve ATS compatibility.
questions.answer: Applicant Tracking System -- software scanning resumes for keywords before human review. Practices: (1) Use standard headers (Experience, Education, Skills) not creative alternatives. (2) Avoid tables, columns, graphics that ATS cannot parse.

quizzes.topic: Project Management, IP Law, and Contracts

questions.question: Your studio is 3 weeks from a milestone with 5 weeks of work remaining. As producer, describe specific Agile steps to address this.
questions.answer: 1) Emergency backlog triage: categorize as must-have/should-have/nice-to-have. 2) Identify descope candidates. 3) Look for parallelization. 4) Communicate to stakeholders with options (reduced scope on time vs extension) and recommendation. 5) Update sprint plan with daily tracking.

questions.question: A developer leaves Studio A (where they built terrain generation) for Studio B. What can and cannot they bring?
questions.answer: CANNOT: source code (work-for-hire), documentation, design specs, trade secrets. CAN: general knowledge, publicly available algorithms, tool/language experience. Should check non-compete and invention assignment clauses.

rubric.key: Trade secrets vs general knowledge distinction

questions.question: What is the difference between copyright and trademark in games? Give one example of each.
questions.answer: Copyright protects creative expression (code, art, music) automatically. Trademark protects names/logos/identifiers. Mario Kart's code and artwork are copyrighted. The name 'Mario Kart' is trademarked. The mechanic of kart racing with power-ups is not protectable by either.

questions.question: Why does crunch persist despite evidence it reduces productivity? Identify two systemic causes and one personal strategy for a developer entering the industry.
questions.answer: Causes: (1) Scope creep with fixed deadlines creates a gap crunch fills (project management failure). (2) Hero culture rewards visible sacrifice with promotions. Strategy: In interviews, ask about last crunch period duration and what scope management processes exist.

questions.question: A Steam game sells for $20. Publisher recoups a $500K advance at 50/50 share. How many units before the developer receives royalties?
questions.answer: Net per unit: $20 * 0.70 = $14. Recoup: $500K / $14 = 35,714 units. Developer receives $0 for first ~35,714 units. After recoup: $7/unit (50% of $14).

rubric.key: Correct net revenue after platform fee

questions.question: Write a bug report for: player falls through floor when landing near platform edges from high jumps in a 3D platformer.
questions.answer: Title: Player clips through floor on high-jump edge landings. Severity: Major. Priority: High. Steps: 1) Load Level 3. 2) Jump to max height near platform edge. 3) Land within 0.5 units of edge. Expected: Normal landing. Actual: Falls through geometry (~30% repro rate). Likely collision detection issue at high downward velocity near edge geometry.

questions.question: A Steam Next Fest demo got 50,000 downloads but only 3,000 wishlists (6% conversion vs 15-20% benchmark). Diagnose and propose two improvements.
questions.answer: The demo attracted attention but failed to convince. Likely: poor demo content selection, no compelling hook at ending, or quality issues damaging impressions. Actions: (1) Restructure demo to end at a cliffhanger with wishlist CTA. (2) Add post-demo survey identifying specific non-wishlist reasons.

questions.question: Explain psychological safety in game dev teams. Describe one practice that creates it and one that destroys it.
questions.answer: Psychological safety: shared belief that the team is safe for risk-taking without humiliation. Creates: retrospectives where leaders acknowledge own mistakes first. Destroys: publicly blaming individuals for bugs in team meetings, teaching everyone to hide problems.

questions.question: Why do most new game studios fail within 5 years? Identify the most common cause and one preventive measure.
questions.answer: Most common: underfunding combined with over-scoping first project. Studios attempt dream games requiring 5x their budget. Prevention: scope first project to ship in 6-12 months on existing funds. A small shipped game proves execution ability and generates revenue.

questions.question: Name two systemic barriers to gender diversity in game industry leadership and one specific studio-level action addressing each.
questions.answer: Barrier 1: Pipeline -- limited early exposure for women. Action: sponsor game development workshops with Girls Who Code. Barrier 2: Hostile workplace culture driving women out. Action: enforce code of conduct with clear reporting and consequences, plus anonymous climate surveys.

questions.question: You have two offers: (a) QA at AAA studio, $55K, clear promotion path; (b) game designer at 5-person startup, $40K + equity. What factors matter for a 5-year perspective?
questions.answer: Factors: (1) Financial viability at $40K. (2) Learning: startup = immediate responsibility, limited mentorship; AAA = structured learning, industry network. (3) Career trajectory: AAA QA path takes 2-3 years; startup title is immediate but needs portfolio proof. (4) Startup viability: funding runway, founding team track record.

questions.question: What is the T-shaped professional concept and why is it valuable in games? Give an example.
questions.answer: T-shaped: deep expertise in one discipline (vertical) plus broad knowledge across adjacent areas (horizontal). Valuable because game dev requires cross-discipline collaboration. Example: systems designer with deep economy/progression expertise plus broad Unity programming, 3D art pipeline, and UX knowledge.

rubric.key: Concrete example with depth and breadth

major_assignments.title: Professional Portfolio and Career Strategy Package
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete professional package: polished portfolio website with 3+ case studies, industry-targeted resume, tailored cover letter for a real posting, and 5-year career strategy with contingency plans.
objectives: Build a professional portfolio with discipline-appropriate case studies
Write ATS-compatible resume and tailored cover letter for a real posting
Articulate a realistic career strategy informed by industry research
Demonstrate readiness through STAR responses and portfolio walkthrough preparation
deliverables: Live portfolio website URL with 3+ case studies
Cover letter for a specific current posting (link required)

criteria: Work samples relevant to target discipline
Tailored cover letter with company research evidence
Realistic target roles with industry research support
Skills gap analysis with actionable development plans
Networking plan with concrete actions and timeline
Complete professional package demonstrating job-search readiness
Portfolio case studies communicating skills and process clearly
Career strategy grounded in research and honest self-assessment
Evidence of iteration through peer review

major_assignments.title: Studio Simulation: Agile Game Production Sprint
major_assignments.description: Teams of 4-5 produce a playable prototype over five two-week sprints using full Scrum methodology with all ceremonies, backlog management, velocity tracking, and retrospective documentation.
objectives: Execute multi-sprint Agile process from concept to prototype
Manage scope, priorities, and team dynamics
deliverables: Sprint planning docs for all 5 sprints with velocity data
Individual contribution logs and peer evaluations

criteria: All ceremonies conducted and documented every sprint
Retrospective actions implemented in subsequent sprints
Appropriate scope for team and timeline
Sprint reports with meaningful data and insights
Full Agile process with authentic ceremonies
Playable prototype with disciplined scope management
Effective team collaboration with documented improvement

major_assignments.title: Industry Analysis and Professional Readiness Capstone
major_assignments.description: Comprehensive analysis of a game industry sector or trend, combined with updated career strategy, professional pitch video, and reflective self-assessment demonstrating readiness to enter the industry.
objectives: Deep understanding of a specific industry sector through analysis
Connect industry trends to career planning implications
Produce a polished professional pitch for networking
Synthesize course learning into professional readiness assessment
deliverables: Updated career strategy reflecting analysis insights

criteria: Comprehensive sector overview with data and sources
Key players and dynamics accurately described
Trends with evidence and projected implications
Opportunities and threats connected to career implications
Skill plan addresses gaps from analysis
Networking targets informed by sector knowledge
Contingency plans account for identified threats
Would leave positive impression at networking event
Honest about strengths and development areas
Connects specific course experiences to growth
Rigorous industry analysis with deep understanding
Career strategy informed by analytical insights

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes assessing industry knowledge, project management, legal concepts, professional ethics, and career readiness

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on exercises in portfolio development, resume writing, sprint planning, contract analysis, and marketing planning

major_assignments.description: Three comprehensive projects: professional portfolio package, studio simulation sprint, and industry analysis capstone

midterm_examination.description: Mixed format exam covering industry structure, project management, intellectual property, and professional ethics

final_project.description: Capstone industry analysis and career strategy demonstrating professional readiness

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all guest speaker sessions, mock interviews, and team sprint ceremonies. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late individual assignments are penalized 10% per day. Sprint deliverables cannot be submitted late as they depend on team cadence. Extensions require advance arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Portfolio and career strategy assignments are individual work. The studio simulation is a required team project with peer evaluation contributing to individual grades.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All portfolio work must be your own or properly attributed. Resume content must be truthful. Fabricating experience, credentials, or project contributions will result in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a project management tool (Trello or Jira free tier), web hosting for portfolio (GitHub Pages, Netlify, or similar), and game development tools for the studio simulation.
